symptoms.symptom_name: Uniform erythema confined to sun-exposed skin with sharp demarcation at clothing line

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A sharply demarcated erythema following the sun-exposed distribution is characteristic of a UV burn.
PLE typically produces discrete papules/plaques on some exposed areas rather than confluent uniform erythema across all exposed skin.

symptoms.symptom_name: Intense cutaneous heat, stinging pain and tightness of the skin

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Heat, stinging and a tight/drawing sensation reflect the inflammatory vasodilatation and interstitial oedema of a superficial burn.

symptoms.symptom_name: Onset 4-6 hours after prolonged unprotected UV exposure

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Delayed onset a few hours after unprotected sun exposure is the typical time course of UVB erythema.
PLE often appears over hours-to-days and recurrently, whereas this is a single acute post-exposure episode.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of blistering — skin dry and intact, not weeping

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of blisters and an intact, dry epidermis is required to exclude a partial-thickness burn and confirm a superficial first-degree burn.
An intact non-blistered epidermis defines a first-degree (superficial) burn.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of systemic heat-illness features (no confusion, headache, nausea, fever or rigors)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal mentation and the absence of headache, nausea, fever and rigors are required to exclude systemic heat illness after significant sun exposure.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of dehydration features (normal urine output, no postural dizziness, drinking normally)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Maintained urine output and absence of postural symptoms argue against significant dehydration accompanying heat illness.

symptoms.symptom_name: Brisk, complete blanching of erythema on light pressure

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Brisk complete blanching indicates intact superficial dermal capillaries — a superficial (first-degree) burn; sealed clinician examination finding, not obtainable in chat.
Deeper burns show sluggish or absent blanching; brisk blanching argues against partial-thickness injury.

symptoms.symptom_name: Skin hot to touch with intact epidermis and no oedema on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Hot, intact, non-oedematous skin is consistent with a superficial inflammatory burn; requires clinician palpation, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal clinician-measured vitals, moist mucous membranes and normal neurological examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal temperature, heart rate, hydration and neurology exclude systemic heat illness — a clinician-measured set sealed from the AI Doctor, which must reason from the patient's systemic screen instead.
Poor response to paracetamol alone reflects the inflammatory nature of burn pain (NSAID cover added) rather than discriminating between diagnoses.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Sun-exposed demarcated erythema + delayed post-exposure onset + no blistering + brisk complete blanching together define an uncomplicated superficial first-degree burn, distinguishing it from a deeper partial-thickness injury.
A negative systemic screen (no CNS features, no dehydration, normal vitals) excludes heat exhaustion/heat stroke and supports safe community management of the cutaneous burn.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated for an uncomplicated first-degree sunburn with no systemic features — this is a clinical diagnosis; routine bloods would be over-investigation.
No broken skin or signs of infection — swabbing intact erythematous skin is unnecessary.
